title: MIBE Excel Corner
text:
Welcome to the MIBE Excel Corner! This page is dedicated to all the key concepts, notes, and resources you need for mastering the basics of MS Excel in the Business Analytics with Excel course. Whether you’re just starting or refining your skills, you’ll find everything you need.
There are two types of pages here: - Class Notes containing explanation of the main concepts, links to official documentation, and use cases. - Exercise Instructions containing videos and step-by-step instructions to practice each week’s topics.


The skills presented in this portion of the class form the foundation of effective Excel usage for business students and professionals. Mastering these fundamental techniques enables users to work more efficiently with data, reducing time spent on repetitive tasks while improving accuracy and presentation quality.



Excel proficiency is essential in modern business environments where data-driven decision-making dominates. The skills demonstrated in these videos address core business needs: data collection and organization, numerical analysis, visual presentation, and efficient workflow management. Business students who master these techniques gain the ability to analyze financial data, create professional reports, manage large datasets, and communicate insights effectively through visual formatting.




The techniques covered in this class dramatically reduce manual work. Instead of typing the same data 25 times or formatting cells individually, you will learn to leverage Excel’s built-in automation features. These time-saving methods will allow you to focus on analysis and interpretation rather than data entry and formatting.




Beyond efficiency, these skills enable users to create visually appealing, easy-to-interpret spreadsheets. Conditional formatting and data bars transform raw numbers into visual insights that stakeholders can quickly understand. Professional formatting enhances credibility and makes complex data more accessible to non-technical audiences.
Let’s dive in and sharpen those Excel skills!

title: Lesson 1 - Introduction to Formatting
text: Microsoft Excel is the most widely used data-processing application in the world, and its influence in organizations continues to grow. There is a good reason for this as Excel is extremely powerful - if you know how to use it. In all but a few professions your employer will expect you to be comfortable analyzing data using Microsoft Excel. If you join any kind of business your Excel skills will set you apart - for better or worse! You are taking this course to become a sophisticated end-user. Thus, mastery, rather than familiarity, should be your goal. Mastery should be your focus for two reasons. Philosophically, if you are going to do something you should do it well. Pragmatically, you realize that the competencies you acquire in Excel will very much influence the level of success you will achieve in your career. Let’s roll up our sleeves and get started!

title: Lesson 1 - Working with Workbooks
text:
Tasks and Steps

Open a new Excel workbook.
Copy only the top 25 pizza companies from “PizzaToday”

Make sure to include the header row of the table.

Recommended: Use Chrome browser for copying.

Place the data into Sheet1 of the workbook, starting from cell A1.

title: Lesson 1 - Working with Workbooks
text:
A workbook is the main file in Excel that contains one or more worksheets. When you open Excel, you’re working with a workbook, typically saved with an .xlsx extension. Think of a workbook as a binder that holds multiple pages (worksheets) of data.
Official documentation: Basic tasks in Excel




A cell is the basic unit of a worksheet, defined by the intersection of a column and a row (e.g., A1, B5). Cells can contain text, numbers, or formulas.
Official documentation: - Cell basics - Create or change a cell reference - CELL function




Rows run horizontally (numbered 1, 2, 3…), columns vertically (labeled A, B, C…). The intersection point is a cell.
Official documentation: Refer to Rows and Columns




Ctrl+C copies selected content; Ctrl+V pastes it. Works across Excel and most software.
Official documentation: Keyboard shortcuts in Excel




Hyperlinks are links in cells that point to websites, files, or workbook locations. Remove them by right-clicking → “Remove Hyperlink”.
Official documentation: Remove or turn off hyperlinks




AutoFill extends data series (like numbers, dates) by dragging the fill handle (corner of a cell). Excel recognizes patterns and continues sequences.
Official documentation: Fill data automatically in worksheet cells




Copies formatting from one cell/range and applies to another. Single-click for one use; double-click to keep active for multiple uses.
Official documentation: Use Format Painter




Font formatting changes text appearance. Ctrl+B makes text bold. Font size increases/decreases text size.
Official documentation: Format cells




Excel stores dates as numbers, displaying them based on chosen format (Long Date, Short Date, Custom). Use Ctrl+1 to format.

Official documentation: - Format numbers as dates - Number format codes




Ctrl+S saves the workbook. If new, prompts for file name/location.
Official documentation: Save your workbook




Paste Special gives advanced paste options: values only, formulas, formats, and more. Access via Ctrl+Alt+V or right-click.
Official documentation: - Paste Special - Paste options




Adds a range of cells, e.g., =SUM(A1:A10). Can also sum multiple separate cells or ranges.
Official documentation: SUM function




AutoSum (Σ) quickly inserts SUM (and other functions) into a selected cell, automatically detecting the range. Shortcut: Alt+=
Official documentation: Use AutoSum to sum numbers




A sheet is a single page in a workbook. Tabs at the bottom of Excel let you organize/use multiple sheets in one file.
Official documentation: - Select worksheets - Insert or delete a worksheet




Relative references (e.g., A1) shift automatically when formulas are copied to new locations.
Official documentation: Switch between relative, absolute, and mixed references




Absolute references (e.g., $A$1) stay fixed when formulas are copied. Add $ before letter or number as needed.
Official documentation: Switch between relative, absolute, and mixed references




The bar above the worksheet shows/edit cell content or formulas, even if the cell displays a result.
Official documentation: Overview of formulas in Excel




Calculates the arithmetic mean of specified numbers/range.
Official documentation: AVERAGE function




Add lines around cells to improve visual organization (no effect on calculations).
Official documentation: Apply or remove cell borders




Displays values as percentages by multiplying by 100 and adding %. Use % button on the ribbon.
Official documentation: Format numbers as percentages




Organize files in folders, use clear names, move from Downloads, and save frequently.
Official documentation: Save your workbook




Apply color, icons, or data bars to cells automatically based on rules (e.g., highlight values >100).
Official documentation: Use conditional formatting




Conditional formatting presets to highlight cells >, <, =, text contains, or duplicates.
Official documentation: Use conditional formatting




Conditional formatting rules can be managed, edited, or cleared using the Rules Manager.
Official documentation: Manage rule precedence




Visually represent values using horizontal bars inside cells—a quick way to compare values.
Official documentation: Use data bars




Highlight top/bottom items, top/bottom %, above/below average.
Official documentation: Use conditional formatting




Highlight repeated values within a range—useful for identifying data entry errors or duplicates.
Official documentation: Use conditional formatting




Locate and replace text or values across cells/workbook. Shortcut: Ctrl+H.
Official documentation: Find or replace text and numbers




Use formatting, colors, and graphics to make data patterns and trends visible for quick insights.
Official documentation: Use conditional formatting

title: Lesson 1 - Introduction to Formatting
text:
Microsoft Excel is the most widely used data-processing application in the world, and its influence in organizations continues to grow. There is a good reason for this as Excel is extremely powerful - if you know how to use it. In all but a few professions your employer will expect you to be comfortable analyzing data using Microsoft Excel. If you join any kind of business your Excel skills will set you apart - for better or worse! You are taking this course to become a sophisticated end-user. Thus, mastery, rather than familiarity, should be your goal. Mastery should be your focus for two reasons. Philosophically, if you are going to do something you should do it well. Pragmatically, you realize that the competencies you acquire in Excel will very much influence the level of success you will achieve in your career. Let’s roll up our sleeves and get started!



Be efficient – Get stuff done quickly
Be effective – Use the right skills for the right tasks Skills
Create new Excel workbooks and work on multiple worksheets
Insert data in Excel and perform basic calculations
Format cells according to their contents
Use absolute, relative, and mixed referencing
Use conditional formatting
Find and replace content in your worksheet




Being efficient in Excel will save you a significant amount of time over the duration of your career. If you have ever opened Excel you know that the application presents a big canvas of rows and columns to you. This is both a blessing and a curse. On the one hand, you have full control and lots of freedom to perform your analysis. On the other hand, as with any programming task, there many different ways to perform an analysis and it is critical that you spend time up front designing an efficient approach to it. That means that learning Excel is not just about learning where to click, rather it is about learning how to create structured solutions to analytical problems.
Insights

It is likely that if you ask ten different people to perform an analysis in Excel, they will come up with ten different procedures to achieve it. Such variability is possible because Excel provides an incredible number of features – most of which basic users will never hear about. Efficiently using the available instruments in Excel is critical. While you will develop your unique style as an analyst, efficiency should always drive the design of your workbooks. As a general rule, you want to perform analyses that are parsimonious and dynamic.

Your analysis is parsimonious when you minimize the number of calculations and activities.
Your analysis is dynamic when your workbook is not specific to your data but it is robust to data changes. That is, it will not stop working abruptly if the data changes.

A critical differentiator between sophisticated and unsophisticated Excel users is the habit to always use keyboard shortcuts. The earlier you pick up this habit, the better it is. Here are the shortcuts for your platform. Mac Win
There are some important functionalities that are only accessible through shortcuts. In addition to keyboard shortcuts, Excel offers plenty of mouse shortcuts such as auto-fill series and auto columns adjustment. Over the following sessions, we will highlight the most important ones. However, as a general rule, any time that you find yourself performing a repetitive task (like manually input the days of the week) find out the efficient approach. We guarantee you that it exists!
Creating an Excel worksheet is like programming. The idea of a ‘variable’ is central. Variables are the backbone of any program and application, and Excel is no exemption. In simple terms, a variable is a way to store information for later use. Every cell in Excel is basically a variable. You reference it by its column name and row number (e.g., A3).




In your professional life, you will often be asked to perform analyses to share with your employees, colleagues or the boss. Creating professional documents is not only visually appealing, but it is also critical for communication. Creating professional documents is critical to convey the results of complex analyses. Well-formatted, professional spreadsheets get your point across – and they look good doing it! As the designer of the workbook you will have great flexibility, it will be your call whether to present data as a plain table, a colorful one, or a compelling graph. Remember at all times that the design choices you make when sharing your analysis will impact your reader’s opinion of your content (and of your abilities).
Insights

Once you share a report with a colleague or boss you lose control of it. It might happen that, especially if it is an important analysis, your colleagues will share it themselves with other colleagues, and your results might end up in a PowerPoint presentation of a different department. If you are good at what you do, then many people will see that. If you are not that good, well, they will know.
Ultimately your peers will judge you based on the quality of what you produce. This means that you need to deliver reliable analyses presented in a manner that is easily understandable by others. Note that, while a quality presentation does not make up for errors in your data and results, a poor presentation devalues the best of insights. Don’t be your own worst enemy by doing sloppy work or by using the wrong instruments.
You might still think that Excel is just an instrument to perform calculations. In this course, you will see that Excel is much more. It is an instrument for ‘Data Storytelling’ and persuasion. When you enter the job market, as a knowledge worker, you will be expected to explain, enlighten, and engage your audience by providing compelling ‘data driven’ stories.




Objective In this module, we start using Excel. Before learning its advanced functionalities, it is necessary that you are efficient and effective with the basics. To give continuity to the learning we will use an ongoing scenario.
Scenario The idea came to you last semester. As you were cramming hard for your finals, the though hit you – Pizza Delivery by Drones, that could be a real business! You don’t know how it came to you. Maybe it’s because you had a passion for airplanes since you were a kid. Maybe it’s because you have owned all of the DJI quadcopters since the Phantom 1. Or maybe just because you were very hungry and had very little time to spare during finals week. You even have a name in mind for your firm: Papa Drones.
Over the holiday break you saw your older cousing Anna, who is a partner at a large San Francisco based venture capital firm. You told her you wanted to ask your Information Systems professor for some help, but she told you that no serious person would listen to you before you did some preliminary analysis. Anna was intrigued by the idea and was willing to help you a bit in her spare time. She said:
“if you are serious about this idea Microsoft Excel is the instrument of choice to do the analysis.”
“Of course!” you responded.
In this module, we start using Excel. Before learning its advanced functionalities, it is necessary that you are efficient and effective with the basics. To give continuity to the learning we will use an ongoing scenario.

title: Lesson 1 - Introduction to Formatting
text: Microsoft Excel is the most widely used data-processing application in the world, and its influence in organizations continues to grow. There is a good reason for this as Excel is extremely powerful - if you know how to use it. In all but a few professions your employer will expect you to be comfortable analyzing data using Microsoft Excel. If you join any kind of business your Excel skills will set you apart - for better or worse! You are taking this course to become a sophisticated end-user. Thus, mastery, rather than familiarity, should be your goal. Mastery should be your focus for two reasons. Philosophically, if you are going to do something you should do it well. Pragmatically, you realize that the competencies you acquire in Excel will very much influence the level of success you will achieve in your career. Let’s roll up our sleeves and get started!

title: Lesson 1 - Introduction to Formatting
text:

Be efficient – Get stuff done quickly
Be effective – Use the right skills for the right tasks Skills

Insert data in Excel and perform basic calculations
Format cells according to their contents
Use absolute, relative, and mixed referencing
Use conditional formatting
Find and replace content in your worksheet

title: Lesson 1 - Introduction to Formatting
section: Be Efficient - Get stuff done quickly
text:
Be Efficient - Get stuff done quickly
Being efficient in Excel will save you a significant amount of time over the duration of your career. If you have ever opened Excel you know that the application presents a big canvas of rows and columns to you. This is both a blessing and a curse. On the one hand, you have full control and lots of freedom to perform your analysis. On the other hand, as with any programming task, there many different ways to perform an analysis and it is critical that you spend time up front designing an efficient approach to it. That means that learning Excel is not just about learning where to click, rather it is about learning how to create structured solutions to analytical problems.
Insights

It is likely that if you ask ten different people to perform an analysis in Excel, they will come up with ten different procedures to achieve it. Such variability is possible because Excel provides an incredible number of features – most of which basic users will never hear about. Efficiently using the available instruments in Excel is critical. While you will develop your unique style as an analyst, efficiency should always drive the design of your workbooks. As a general rule, you want to perform analyses that are parsimonious and dynamic.

Your analysis is parsimonious when you minimize the number of calculations and activities.
Your analysis is dynamic when your workbook is not specific to your data but it is robust to data changes. That is, it will not stop working abruptly if the data changes.

A critical differentiator between sophisticated and unsophisticated Excel users is the habit to always use keyboard shortcuts. The earlier you pick up this habit, the better it is. Here are the shortcuts for your platform. Mac Win
There are some important functionalities that are only accessible through shortcuts. In addition to keyboard shortcuts, Excel offers plenty of mouse shortcuts such as auto-fill series and auto columns adjustment. Over the following sessions, we will highlight the most important ones. However, as a general rule, any time that you find yourself performing a repetitive task (like manually input the days of the week) find out the efficient approach. We guarantee you that it exists!
Creating an Excel worksheet is like programming. The idea of a ‘variable’ is central. Variables are the backbone of any program and application, and Excel is no exemption. In simple terms, a variable is a way to store information for later use. Every cell in Excel is basically a variable. You reference it by its column name and row number (e.g., A3).

title: Lesson 1 - Introduction to Formatting
section: Be Effective - Get stuff done right
text:
Be Effective - Get stuff done right
In your professional life, you will often be asked to perform analyses to share with your employees, colleagues or the boss. Creating professional documents is not only visually appealing, but it is also critical for communication. Creating professional documents is critical to convey the results of complex analyses. Well-formatted, professional spreadsheets get your point across – and they look good doing it! As the designer of the workbook you will have great flexibility, it will be your call whether to present data as a plain table, a colorful one, or a compelling graph. Remember at all times that the design choices you make when sharing your analysis will impact your reader’s opinion of your content (and of your abilities).
Insights

Once you share a report with a colleague or boss you lose control of it. It might happen that, especially if it is an important analysis, your colleagues will share it themselves with other colleagues, and your results might end up in a PowerPoint presentation of a different department. If you are good at what you do, then many people will see that. If you are not that good, well, they will know.
Ultimately your peers will judge you based on the quality of what you produce. This means that you need to deliver reliable analyses presented in a manner that is easily understandable by others. Note that, while a quality presentation does not make up for errors in your data and results, a poor presentation devalues the best of insights. Don’t be your own worst enemy by doing sloppy work or by using the wrong instruments.
You might still think that Excel is just an instrument to perform calculations. In this course, you will see that Excel is much more. It is an instrument for ‘Data Storytelling’ and persuasion. When you enter the job market, as a knowledge worker, you will be expected to explain, enlighten, and engage your audience by providing compelling ‘data driven’ stories.

title: Lesson 1 - Introduction to Formatting
text:
Exercise Overview
Objective In this module, we start using Excel. Before learning its advanced functionalities, it is necessary that you are efficient and effective with the basics. To give continuity to the learning we will use an ongoing scenario.
Scenario The idea came to you last semester. As you were cramming hard for your finals, the though hit you – Pizza Delivery by Drones, that could be a real business! You don’t know how it came to you. Maybe it’s because you had a passion for airplanes since you were a kid. Maybe it’s because you have owned all of the DJI quadcopters since the Phantom 1. Or maybe just because you were very hungry and had very little time to spare during finals week. You even have a name in mind for your firm: Papa Drones.
Over the holiday break you saw your older cousing Anna, who is a partner at a large San Francisco based venture capital firm. You told her you wanted to ask your Information Systems professor for some help, but she told you that no serious person would listen to you before you did some preliminary analysis. Anna was intrigued by the idea and was willing to help you a bit in her spare time. She said:
“if you are serious about this idea Microsoft Excel is the instrument of choice to do the analysis.”
“Of course!” you responded.
In this module, we start using Excel. Before learning its advanced functionalities, it is necessary that you are efficient and effective with the basics. To give continuity to the learning we will use an ongoing scenario.

title: Lesson 1 - Introduction to Formatting
text:
Tasks and Steps

Open the Starter workbook.
Copy only the top 25 pizza companies from “PizzaToday”

Make sure to include the header row of the table.
Recommended: Use Chrome browser for copying.

Place the data into Sheet1 of the workbook, starting from cell A1.
Adjust the data after pasting:

Remove the hyperlink for each company name in column B.
Fill missing values in column A by completing the ranking. Use the drag handle, do not manually enter each number.
Resize each column to make all contents visible.

Format the worksheet:

Apply the style from cell B2 to all text in columns B, D, and E (use Format Painter).
Set the header row as bold, font size 16 pt.
All other cells: Calibri font, size 12 pt.

Create the date column:

Type Date in cell H1, formatted like the other headers.
In H2, enter: 12/31/2015
Use Autofill to generate the same date for all following rows.
Format the dates in column H to display: Thursday, December 31, 2015

Save the file as username_e1a.xlsx and close it.

title: Practice 1d - Conditional formatting
text:
Tasks and Steps

Download and open the empty starter file.
An important Excel feature is Conditional Formatting. Mac Win It is a powerful functionality to highlight relevant information. For example, in the Calculations worksheet:


Highlight in red all the chains that have more than 100 units (column B).
Highlight in green all the chains that have more than 100,000,000 dollars in gross sales (column C).
Highlight in yellow all the dates that are a duplicate (column F). A duplicate is a value that appears more than once in the column.


After you apply the above steps you realize that too much conditional formatting is hampering your design. You basically, end up not highlighting anything when everything is highlighted. You can resolve this problem:


Modify the conditional formatting in column B. Use 500 the threshold (i.e., the number of units above which you apply the formatting).
Modify the conditional formatting of column C. Update the gross sales amount to 500,000,000 dollars. What did you notice?
Clear the conditional formatting for the dates (column F). They are all the same.


On the Calculations worksheet do the following:


Format column D using blue Data Bars (select the Solid Fill option).
Format column E using a Top/bottom rule that highlights only the values above the average (use green as the color). Mac Win


Use find and replace to change all dates to 01/01/2016. Try to perform this step using the keyboard shortcuts and updating all the dates simultaneously. Mac Win
Save the file and close it.

title: Practice 1c - Data Import and Cleaning
text:
Tasks and Steps

Download and open the starter file.
In cell F27, use the function SUM to compute the total of column F. Mac Win
In cell G27, calculate the total of column G. Use AutoSum to complete this task. Mac Win
Copy, simultaneously, the headers and data in columns B, F and G (do not copy the total, just the data and column headers).


Paste the copied data in cell A1 of Sheet2. Mac Win
Resize the columns to show all content.
Rename Sheet1 as Data and Sheet2 as Calculations. Mac Win
Make sure the sheets are in the correct order. The first one is the Data worksheet, the second is the Calculations worksheet.


In column D of the Calculations worksheet compute the Gross Sales per unit as: Gross Sales divided by Units.


The column header should read: Unit Sales.
Make sure the heading is formatted consistently with the other ones.
Notice that in this case relative references are sufficient to compute the result for each row (be efficient, drag the formula). Mac Mac Win


In the Calculations worksheet type Total in A27 and Average in A28.


Format both cells in bold.
Copy the formula from cell F27 in the Data worksheet and paste it into cell B27 of the Calculations worksheet.
Copy the formula from cell G27 in the Data worksheet and paste it into cell C27 of the Calculations worksheet.
Make sure the results are based on the data available in the Calculations worksheet. Delete the total from the Data worksheet. If you have used the correct pasting option, you will not see any error in the Calculations worksheet. Congratulations!!


Calculate the Average number of units (column B) and the Average gross sales (column C). Use AutoSum. Mac Win
Use a Top Border to visually separate the totals from the data. Make sure to apply the border only to cells A27:C27. Mac Win
In column E of the Calculations worksheet calculate the gross sales proportion of each restaurant chain over the total gross sales for all chains (Gross Sales divided by Total).


The column header should read: Sales Percentage. It should be consistently formatted with the other ones.
Make sure to correctly use relative references and absolute references.
Format the data using the Percent style with no decimal value. Mac Win


Save the file and close it.
